div.h1: Performance Analysis: Single MPMC vs. Sharded Queue
div.h3: Hardware Level: Multi-Core Cache Contention & Throughput Scaling

g.text: Architecture A: Single MPMC Ring Buffer (Global Atomic)

div.p: Architectural Summary In a Single MPMC queue, the head and tail pointers often reside on the same or adjacent 64-byte cache lines. When multiple cores perform compare_and_swap (CAS), the MESI protocol forces cache line ownership to move across the interconnect. This "Cache Line Bouncing" causes the memory bus to stall and throughput to drop as core count increases.
Sharding solves this via isolation. By assigning core-local shards and ensuring 64-byte padding between them, atomic updates stay local to the core's private cache, enabling linear scaling.

ul.li:
Single MPMC (8 Cores): 14.2 μs
Reason: MESI protocol 'Inval' storms during atomic cross-core sync.

Sharded (8 Cores): 0.8 μs
Reason: No cross-core cache line bouncing; CPU stays in L1/L2.

g.title: Critical Contention: Head and Tail on same line causes MESI Invalidate storms
Critical Contention: Head and Tail on same line causes MESI Invalidate storms
